segments.text: Good morning, Minister and officials.
Welcome to the Social Services and Community Select Committee.
Thank you for coming back again this year.
Look, just the normal process. If you could introduce yourself
maybe for those watching, and then give us some
opening comments, and then we'll move to questions from members.
Yep, thank you.
So look, at the table today, I have with me the Chief Executive,
Amanda Miller,
and also in the room I've got Oranga Tamariki leadership team to
assist with answering any operational questions today.
Look, during this term as Children's Minister, it's been a real
privilege to be able to go up and down the country,
and to manage to visit all the Oranga Tamariki
sites across the country. Sometimes, some of them I've
been to multiple times. And in these visits, I've also been able
to, on average, visit around three other community provider partners
on the day as well. These visits have allowed me to
have a real insight into
what the frontline are feeling,
the issues that they are facing each and every day, and just
their commitment to their job. Often they can do everything
right
and not get the result that they would like, and they get up the next day and they
do it all over again. And that takes courage, passion, and conviction
of people that really do care about the outcomes of our young people.
So I just wanted to take a moment to thank them for the amazing efforts
that they've made.
And I'd also like to acknowledge the caregivers, because I've also had the
opportunity to meet caregivers from across the range.
So whether they be Oranga Tamariki caregivers, provider caregivers,
caregivers in the disability space, and also just those
whanau caregivers that get up each and every day as well, those
grandparents, aunties, uncles, that step up when a
young person needs them the most. So it's been an absolute privilege
to be able to do that in my time as the Children's Minister, and I look
forward to continuing doing that.
The size of the Oranga Tamariki
is too big.
We can't understate how massive the job is.
On average, Oranga Tamariki staff now support or interact
with around 18,000 young people every day.
Oranga Tamariki receives 108,000 reports of concern around
the safety of a child per year. That's roughly one every five
minutes, every day. Each of these needs to be investigated for
abuse and neglect. Safety planning needs to be undertaken.
Families in crisis need to be worked with to ensure that they're referred to
the services that can best help them.
On top of this, social workers need to run family group conferences, meet
family court obligations, engage in youth justice work, recruit
and support caregivers, oversee adoptions, manage complex and
high-risk situations involving children in immediate risk
of harm. Oranga Tamariki staff also run and oversee care and protection
residences, youth justice residences, community-based
residential care homes, and community-based youth justice homes,
including for serious youth offenders.
These are highly structured and highly demanding environments, dealing
with some of the most challenging and high-risk young people in the
country, where safety, rehabilitation, and accountability
all must be managed at once in real time.
And for the children who do enter care, the work continues well beyond
placement. It involves securing safe homes, managing
transitions, supporting education engagement, ensuring access to
health and mental health services, connecting young people with drug and alcohol
supports where needed, and building long-term pathways so they're not left
without support after trauma. This is a demanding, complex, and
often emotionally draining work.
It requires constant judgment in high-risk situations where the
consequences of getting it wrong can be life-changing.
I have absolute respect for all the social workers, residential
staff, youth justice workers, caregivers, and frontline teams who show
up every day.
The budget initiatives this year have been put together from the voices
of the frontline, from what they've told us are their major concerns.
The total 2026 budget package for Oranga Tamariki
brings $212.8 million over five
years, as well as $800,000 of capital funding across
these areas of government priorities.
$93.5 million operating and $800,000
capital expenditure funding over four years for additional staffed care
placements for
the increasing numbers of children with high and complex needs that we are seeing.
It will also enhance the range of care responses and intensive clinical
services to prevent escalation of needs and placement
breakdowns, providing better outcomes for children and
families. $90.5 million over four years to
respond to an increased number of reports of concern, and to
implement efficiency measures that improve delivery
timeliness. $27.7 million over two years to
progress the implementation of the recommendations made in the Karen
Potasi Review. Funding will enable Oranga Tamariki to respond
to increased demand for services, including increased reports of concern
and family court applications. And $1.4 million
over five years to address the implementation costs for Oranga
Tamariki arising from the resolution agreement for the Stanford
versus Attorney General case.
This funding will meet costs arising from the transfer of land and assets under
the resolution agreement and the leaseback to Oranga Tamariki.
Overall,
I have reviewed my ministerial priorities in
2026 and reconfirmed the priority performance
indicators that Oranga Tamariki will publicly report against.
These include the percentage of children in care who have been visited by their
social worker at least once in the last eight weeks to ensure their
ongoing safety and well-being. The percentage of critical, within
24 hours, or very urgent, within 48 hours, reports of
concern, to be addressed within these time
frames. Survey results of the Oranga Tamariki caregivers
report, and proportion of complaints audited that were handled in
a way that fully met Oranga Tamariki standards.
The Oranga Tamariki has published the progress against these ministerial
priorities in the performance reports for quarter one, two, and three in the
financial year of '25, '26. And the published
results show that all but one are on track, that being around
the reports of concern, which is why we have put some more
funding into that space. We have seen an unprecedented
increase in reports of concern during this term, and this budget has seen
a number of significant investments to improve this outcome.
The Oranga Tamariki also reports on the government target for youth offending
to achieve a 15% reduction in the total number of children and young people with
serious and persistent offending.
We achieved and surpassed this goal well in advance of the
target, but we will continue with this work and
improve, thanks to greater collaboration across the government
agencies, and thanks to undertaking the new and meaningful pilots and
programs across the country to address these issues long term.
For every child who comes to the attention of Oranga Tamariki and
needs help, I expect that Oranga Tamariki can
help to make their lives better. I believe that we can, and I
believe that we are.
I am heartened by the real and positive changes we are seeing across the
agency and would now welcome any questions the members have.
Thank you very much, Minister. The first question is Helen Julian.
Tena koe, Minister, and welcome to the new Chief Executive, Amanda.
I want to first ask some questions about youth justice,
and it was really concerning to read the Independent Children's
Monitor report that reported that 50% of rangatahi
Maori involved in youth justice had more than 10 reports of concern
made about them when they were younger.
They note the pathway to prison from care to prison, and it would appear
that Oranga Tamariki isn't doing enough to intervene early.
What is in this year's budget to address this?
Look, you raise a really good point.
And it is distressing to see that some of them have had up to
10 reports,
but that also tells you why this is a problem.
It's a generational problem. It's been going on for a very long time, long
before many of us have even stepped into this place.
And so we have to address both. And the budgets
across the last budget and this budget, the focus is
really in that prevention space as well, making sure that when a young person
with high and complex needs comes through our doors, which is often
one of the trigger points to being in the
system longer, having worse outcomes
because those high and complex needs haven't been either
addressed
or the right care hasn't been put in place, and they do
end up within the justice system. So that's why this investment
into the high and complex needs, making sure that we're assessing their needs,
because how can we say we're meeting their needs if we don't even know what their
needs are? So that's what that investment is for, to
stop those repeat reports of concern.
We've also started up some pilots,
where the local iwi and community
are taking that challenge on. Where the reports of concern go directly to
community, and they realize, and we realize, that the
phone was picked up for a reason. And so we make sure that those
supports and services are wrapped around them with that first report of
concern. And we're finding that we're getting less repeat reports of
concern because that support has been put in earlier.
So those are some of the things that we've been doing.
Do you believe that the Military Style Academy funding,
the millions of dollars that is put into that, is the best use of
taxpayer money and the best way to address these issues raised?
What I do believe is we can't afford not to invest that money.
These young people, for a long time, as you've just stated in your previous
question, have ended up in the youth justice system, and it's been
treated like a holding pen for corrections.
I'm not going to tolerate that. And the Military Style Academy is a
holistic approach to helping rehabilitate these young people so
that they don't end up in corrections.
So my answer to you is yes, I do believe it is a good value
for
the taxpayer, because we will have less young people ending
up in our corrections system, and we'll have more young people that will actually
have a brighter future in life.
How many young people are currently in the
latest Military Style Academy?
I've made it very clear, and I'll make it very clear with that first question,
that we won't be answering questions around young people in the Military Style
Academy. You would've seen that in the written parliamentary questions that you've
had back, that their privacy and their families' privacy and
wellbeing is more important than being used as a political football.
Are there still 10 in the Military Style Academy?
I won't be answering-
Sorry, she can't answer that question.
Will I get-
I won't be answering that question
... next question will have to come-
Supplementary?
We're vetting the pilot
No, no. We move on to covering.
Moria Minister,
I know that I've asked you
some of these questions in question time in the House and through
written questions. So I'd really like to just needle in a little bit
more.
I've got some further questions and some supplementaries as well, Mr.
Chair,
to clarify exactly who is caring for kids in
Oranga Tamariki Care, kids
with highly complex needs, disabilities,
in our system.
Can you confirm that kids in Oranga Tamariki care
system are being cared for by adequately trained staff?
So I can't confirm across the board for every single child
within the care or who have come to the attention of Oranga Tamariki
care. But what I can say is that was a huge concern for me
when I first came into this role, which is why I've put such a large
investment into making sure that we are building up the
capacity and capability of Oranga Tamariki.
But also our community partners and providers and potential
community partners and providers, giving them the skillsets that they need
so that they can provide that critical care for our young
people. I'm not going to sit here and say it's perfect.
But what I will say is there has been a massive effort across the
country, to make sure that the training
and the suitability of our caregivers is the best that it can
be.
Supplementary, thank you. Have you been made aware of any
instances of security guards who may be trained as
security guards, but not
with the skills for
the level of care needed for children,
and especially children in Oranga Tamariki care.
Have you been made aware of any instances-
Sorry, you can't make assumptions in a question.
The question is, has she been made aware.
No, you're saying that security guards didn't necessarily have the training.
You're making assumptions. Just clarify.
There's no assumption-
Thank you, Mr. Chair. I will clarify.
So have you been made aware of any instances where security guards
have been caring for children in Oranga Tamariki care?
No, I've not been made aware of security guards being caregivers to
children in Oranga Tamariki care.
Thank you.
I've not been made aware.
Minister, have you,
after question time and after-
But can I just-
Of course
... Sorry to interrupt. But can I just say, if you have been made
aware of a situation like that,
I'd be very grateful if you sent an email through to my office
so that we can follow that up. Because,
if
I understand correctly, you're saying that security guards
have been put in charge of the care of young people
with no other supports in place, just a security guard.
And I want to make sure that I can follow that up.
So if you can-
Thank you
... please send through to my office any specifics.
And that's a concern that I really have about this process here.
This process here might be a place where you can come and ask questions.
But if you're aware of something like that, I would have expected that it would've
been brought to my attention before coming here, so that I can
deal with the safety of a young person, because that's my role,
that's my job, that's my passion. That's why I get up each and every day, to make
sure that young people are safe. And when I hear stuff where they might not be,
it's actually quite upsetting for me sitting here and listening to the fact
that maybe a member has known that young people aren't safe, and it hasn't been
brought to my attention. So I'd just like to just
clarify, please bring these to my attention.
Thank you so much, Minister. I can see that we're both really
concerned about the safety of children in Aotearoa,
and
that I have brought this up in
question time and also followed up with written questions
and also reached out to your office just to be clear.
So what I would like to ask-
Let's just be clear. The ones that have been followed up through written
questions and questions in the House would've been followed up, and you would've
had answers to them. So I'm talking about if you've
got any scenarios where you know kids are
currently in that situation, please
just let my office know.
Thank you, Minister. So- Going to that point,
did you, after
the questions in the House, the written parliamentary questions, and the
independent children's monitor report, have any verbal
or written
advice sought from your office into
this?
I get a whole lot of advice in my role.
If you want specific answers to that advice, you can put that in writing,
and I can get that back to you. But I'm not going to be able to sit here and quote
specific advice across my role as minister and across
all the questions that have been asked of me as a minister.
I really appreciate that you're- I'll just move on to the last one.
I've just got one more question. Thank you.
Just confirming that,
because it was brought to your attention multiple times, and I understand the
massive workload that you have,
am I to understand that you haven't sought further advice
on these?
Okay. No,
no, no. And can you absolutely- No, that's fine ...
confirm that there are no
staff who
do not have the required training looking after kids in Oranga Tamariki?
My expectation would be that every young person that is in the care of
Oranga Tamariki either has a qualified person who is
taking care of them with the skill set they need, or they are in
placements in homes.
And are you aware of any instances where they aren't? Moving on.
Next question, Lauren. Are you aware of any instances where they aren't? Excuse me.
Sorry.
Thank you. There's $20 million in this budget for
young people with complex needs. Can you please explain what kind
of complex needs they might be and what kind of
programs you envision this budget to be spent on?
So, there's a whole lot of operational work that needs to
go on to make sure that we are meeting the needs of
these high and complex needs that are coming through our door.
I think I'll hand over to Nicolette, who may be able
to talk through the work program of what that looks like and
how that money is going to make a difference in those young people's lives.
So, the specific funding in
this budget is actually about increasing the capacity of our
complex placements for exactly the reason that we
want young people in care with caregivers who can
meet those needs. So, we know our young people have much higher rates of
disability, higher rates of mental health needs.
This is about upskilling. We want all caregivers to be
skilled. We want a particular group of caregivers who have a higher
degree of support and skill around meeting particular needs.
It also funds an increase in our psychologists and
therapeutic resources within Oranga Tamariki, so more
practical support can be provided to the caregivers.
It builds off funding in the previous budget in terms of the integrated safe
care work we're doing, which is in response to
Whanaketia, the royal commission, which is about upskilling
our caregivers across the board,
making our care process easier and simpler for caregivers,
and providing increased visibility and assurance
around safety and a range of things in that space.
Thank you.
Thank you.
New question, Lauren.
Oh, thank you. Thank you, Ms.
Just looking at the categories in the budget, which
is investing in children and young people, there are four
sort of major categories. The first one is the statutory
intervention and transition. Now, I understand most of that work
is done by NGOs, community organizations, et cetera.
But there's very little visibility about how that is
broken down, and therefore very difficult
to assess the performance. So, just wondering if you could comment on
whether there is an opportunity for OT to
break down those categories a little bit more in
budget, and then perhaps mention about how
you measure the performance of those NGOs that
are delivering for you.
So,
just so I've got it right, I don't want to be talking about the wrong thing.
You're talking about disabilities transitioning out of care?
No, no. So, in the budget, investing in children and young people.
Yeah.
So, the bulk of that fund, which is over a billion
dollars, is the statutory intervention.
Yes. Gotcha.
And so, but it's
over 1.1 billion.
Mm.
But it's just one line item.
Yeah.
And so, there's no breakdown of the subcategories.
Yeah.
That makes
the visibility in the allocation, the budget
allocation, difficult to assess.
Yeah. So, I can speak a little bit to that, and then I might give Jesse,
come speak to the changes that are being
made in our contracting and commissioning space
to make sure we're doing exactly what I've just spoken to.
Okay.
And that we're getting really good outcomes for the money that's being spent
on our young people out in the communities, whether it be NGO or our community
partners.
Because we were finding that the contracts that were
existing when I first came in here, and most of them still are
until we transition them over, are based on output,
not outcomes. And so often we didn't actually know if
these young people were getting good services,
or if it's just moderate services, or if they were even receiving the
service at all.
But there is a reporting standard for each contract.
We have hundreds of contracts,
so putting it line by line might be a bit difficult.
But there may be another way that we could report it to get a better
oversight of that. But I'll hand over to Jesse.
Kia ora
Yeah. So approximately 500 million per annum is invested in
contracted services.
Okay.
So through the NGO sector.
And we're happy to follow up in writing about the specific breakdowns as
how that's currently allocated. But roughly speaking, it's
approximately 60% into statutory services-
Right
... and then about 40% into early intervention preventative services.
As the minister said, the performance measures
against all these various different service types do vary.
But we could provide you some insight in writing into primary
performance measures across those contracts.
And a lot of them will pertain to
children having a safe place to stay.
Sure. Yeah.
Or the specific care and protection demands that they require.
My supp was actually going to be about the outcomes-
Yes
... versus outputs.
Yeah.
And I understand the complexity of doing that across
multiple contracts. But, in terms of
our role-
Mm
... it's very difficult to-
Yeah
... to assess how that is performing.
So,
Minister, are you saying that there will be-
Yeah
... some accountability and transparency over
performance in the future?
Yeah. So, as I said, contracts were
generally based on
activities or output,
rather than outcomes.
So with the new framework for commissioning a- and
procurement,
contracts will be changing over to outcomes-based
contracts,
where the discussion can be had between each individual provider
or service around what good outcomes are.
A- and I've learnt, even from talking to community up and down the country,
that this is a concern for them, too.
The way that the contracting environment has been in the past, and
probably for
a long time, is very, very competitive.
And it's based on how many people you get through your door, not based on
actually how are they when they walk out your door.
So it's just making sure that we're getting that balance right, so that
the contracting system isn't based on competitiveness,
it's based on the needs of the kids.
Because far too long,
children and whānau have had to meet the system's demands,
rather than the system meeting the children's and whānau's needs.
So it's flipping that switch, and making sure that we're doing it the right way.
Okay.
A few supps on this, and I might s- kick off with a couple of them.
So I might just read this out. Just Oranga Tamariki said that it
relies extensively on iwi and Māori organizations, non-government organizations,
and social service providers' delivery of a range of services to children and young
people and their families. So it's iwi and Māori organizations, non-government
organizations, and social service providers.
Could you just give a bit of a sense of the scale of that,
the types of organizations and the geographic spread?
Yep.
I would actually love to
introduce Darren,
who has been working really hard over the last two and a
half years around our Enabling Communities program,
which is a real focus on what I've campaigned on and
spoken to around devolution back to community, which I
think is going to be a pathway to better
outcomes for all our whānau and family.
And I think this is a perfect opportunity for Darren to speak to the work that
has been going on, but also the work that's going to continue
to grow over the next budget period.
Sure.
Thank you, Minister. Kia ora koutou.
Just quickly, we are trialing at the moment nine
prototypes. We call them prototypes because we're testing
different kinds of partnerships and outcomes with different
communities. It could be an iwi, it could be a collective of providers,
it could be... So there are nine different types.
We've been working with them quite slowly to some degree because
we're looking at a long-term transformation.
So we want to understand what we need to do as an organization
to be able to support the change. We want to be able to evaluate
what the outcomes are and where the changes are.
So it sits alongside what Jessie talked about, the way that we
contract in services as well. So we've got them with iwi
running across the country. Some of the prototypes are
looking at taking some of our statutory roles and functions,
some that we've never done before, ever, as a ministry.
So that's taken a lot of legal work, a lot of accountability, a lot of
monitoring, and a lot of support for the partner as well.
So we're very mindful that we don't want to transfer some of the
responsibilities without them having the capability.
So we've invested a lot of money in capability over the years to be able
to do that. What we have seen as well, even in commissioning,
so Jessie spoke a little bit around what we're commissioning.
We're also testing models for commissioning, that
iwi might commission services in their own community.
So while the point of commissioning might sound like the interesting point, the
reality is that we're testing what it looks like when iwi or
Māori take leadership and commission what they think that they
need. I think you referred, of course, to the ICM report.
We would start to see that there's disparity for tamariki Māori.
We know that. But where we do good for all children, Māori
outcomes improve as well. But we recognize that there
are some disparities that we need to work for, and that is why we're
working with the nine prototypes across the country.
They identify for us some of the things that we would not have known.
So where we're looking at is how to transform our system.
Is it in the way that we contract services?
Is it in the way that we monitor? What can communities do, and
iwi leadership do, that we can't do? So sorry for the long spiel.
I'll give you a bit about it.
Could you give us an example of, you said new models that have never been tested
before. Can you give us a bit of a flavor of what that looks like?
Yes. I think the ICM report referred to two of them, for example.
So I'll talk about one of them that's already come out public, which is with Ngati
Awa and the Eastern Bay of Plenty Alliance.
They're taking over sections, well, they've applied for us to give
delegation of sections 15 and 17, where they will
end up becoming the core point. So they will be making decisions
on Amanda's behalf as the chief executive.
So it's quite critical. The neat part around that, what
we've also supported and invested in, is that they have created their
own hub of services, where the majority
of government-funded services are coordinated by this iwi
alliance. So families that come through our front door are getting
access to services that they might not otherwise have.
So it's a coordinated service response that Ngati Awa is
providing. So we didn't do that. We've hooked into them and enabled that.
And what we're starting to see as well is that other government agents are seeing
the benefit of that platform from which to grow from.
And that's what we've always believed as well, that meeting the needs of
children is not the responsibility of Oranga Tamariki alone.
There's education, there's health, all of those different providers.
So Oranga Tamariki though is working with partners to partner
lead, which is a big thing for us because we've never done that before, where they
lead it. And so it's taking us a bit longer to understand
how they want to do that and develop. So as a part of that example for you.
Thank you. My last supplementary on this is just
around sort of the earlier point around all the outcome-based contracts.
We actually had an interesting
meeting yesterday as a committee with the Social Investment Agency.
And just a point they made was that it was what you measure that actually feeds
through to what, whether it's outcomes or outputs.
And often if you're just measuring, let's say, a truck roll, you're ultimately
going to be an outputs measurement.
So I'm interested in what measurement,
metrics are you putting in place
to inform these outcomes-based contracts?
There are multiple metrics, so it's also up to the partner that we're working
with as well. But one fundamental one that sits true for all
of them at the moment is that the experience of the children and the
family that comes through the support system.
So it's not just about ticking off the numbers of certain points.
They want to know that experience because they believe that the
experience will sharpen their ability to seek help earlier,
to be able to access support that they otherwise would not, and that they will be
able to get support. So that's one of the measures, is the
satisfaction of whanau and children that are
accessing the services. The other technical ones that we look at as a
ministry is re-notification rates.
So for example, we know that there's a percentage of
families that come through our system, like we heard about the 10
consecutive reports that have come through in the Youth Justice report.
But actually, they keep
coming back into the system. So what we're noticing as
well with some of the pilots or the prototypes that we're testing is that
there's a reduction in the volume of
re-notifications. Which means to say, and I think the ICM report
also kind of identified that there's disparity,
but the further you go into the system, the more persistent that
disparity is. And so for us as well is that what we're
seeing through the Enabling Communities prototypes is a
disruption of some of those patterns.
We haven't yet been able to analyze it, so at the end of this year we will be
evaluating the Enabling Communities prototypes and the
work that's happening across the country on those measures.
Sorry, I did say that was my last one, but I will have to ask one more.
Just in terms of the experience, how do you measure that?
Because that's a qualitative measure,
and if you're talking about a child, they don't really have a comparator often to
compare to. So how do you get a good measurement that you can
actually inform decision-making and-
You're right
... inform whether the performance-based contracting methodology is working?
It is qualitative. It is a qualitative measure.
But we measure that every year as well through our annual
Te Matataki report, which is a survey we undertake with
children that are in our care. So every year they feed into
us. And so every year they tell us that actually their
voices being heard, being recognized,
and being actioned are what they would qualify as us as a
system responding to the needs of young people and children.
So when I talk about how do we qualify experiences of young
people and children, it's that, have they been heard?
Have we noted it? And have we responded to what they have
told us through those reports?
It's always about outcomes though, the outputs.
I'm coming to Donna. Supplementary.
Yeah, I could go on asking questions about this-
Yeah, if you want to, you go for it
... all day. But in terms of the contracts,
I note that in the questions that you supplied back to the
committee,
that in 2025 and 2026, there were 72
contracts awarded over a million dollars.
But it says that the 2026/27 figure is not
available yet because
Oranga Tamariki has not yet finalized what, if any, contracts are
expected to be awarded for the value of $1 million in the
26/27 financial year, which is imminent.
So
I'm just aware that last year there was some delay for some of the organizations in
delivery. They were concerned that this was-
... so they thought their funding was all being cut.
There was a whole lot of drama, and then there was a realization
that contracts had just been delayed, and there was some problem with that.
So
in the face of the challenging times we have for children in our communities, and
if we put them at the center of this, my question is, why are-
Do we not know this? And is there a risk that we end up in the same
situation we ended up in last time?
Well, kia ora. I'm happy to answer that, and I'll hand to Jesse with some of the
detail. So the short answer is that we are
in a very different time than we were a couple of years ago.

in a far greater place. The engagement that Jesse and his team

We are in the process right now of
extending a large number of contracts.

And Jesse, you might be able to talk to some of the numbers there.
Yeah, very good. So yeah,
as the chief executive's mentioned, we are extending large numbers of contracts.
In that process, we are looking for opportunities to
relieve some administrative pressure on providers.

where actually it went from one or maybe up

Right.

so that this new framework fits not only
Oranga Tamariki but fits the community.
So Anisa and the team have been doing a

renew those contracts with certainty without
extending is because that new process needs to kick
in, which gives that longer conversation about what contracts look

that will get good outcomes.

kaimahi.

When it comes to Korowai Manaaki itself,

And just sitting here, I think you said one in every five
minutes of concern.
Yeah.
If you took that across the year.
Yeah.
And obviously that's over a day, when
it's open, those reports will be flooding in.
As a New Zealander, just to hear those numbers are quite shocking.
Mm.
So I'm just wondering, firstly, what
does the ministry understand by those reports of concern?
And obviously, in the last couple years-
Mm
... there's been a lot more concern reports coming in.
Yeah. And so, there's lots of different ways we can look at this, right?
So reports of concern don't necessarily mean
that the young person is at immediate risk of harm.
Sometimes someone will pick up a phone because they have a concern
that maybe this family needs some help.
It could be around their financial circumstances.
It could be around absolutely anything.
But this shows us, with
around 105,000 reports of concern in a
year, show us that more people are actually willing to pick up the phone now,
and have that conversation about caring about their neighbor, caring
about a young child and their life, and actually taking the
time to pick up the phone and have that conversation.
So for me, that side of it is quite positive, because a
hidden problem is really hard to solve.
But a problem that's out in the open and people are willing to have a conversation
about, is easier to try and tackle and
manage.
And we've also got the other stat that kind of surprised me,
around Oranga Tamariki itself, and the staff of Oranga
Tamariki, actually touch point with around
18,000 children a day,
and do something for around 18,000 children a day in some shape or
form.
And so, that just shows the effort that is really going in
to make sure that young people have their needs met.
So we don't just deal with the young people in our care.
We often deal a lot in that prevention space.
And what people don't realize is that the majority of Oranga Tamariki's
work is to prevent young people coming into care.
And I think that point is often lost.
Just supplementary if I may, Mr. Chair.
Mm.
Sure.
In the estimates in the budget-
Mm
... you've allocated considerable funding.
It's over 19 million over the next four years.
And 19.8 million in this year's budget.
What response and what are you expecting to see from that
investment?
Yeah. There have been a lot of reviews and
reports into responses. Karen Potasi review
is one of them. And one of the points that was made within
that review was, that poor young child had so
many different touch points along the way, and he was like
the hidden child.
Because one agency had a concern raised, another one had
a concern raised, but nobody actually followed through with any
action of those. And it's like pieces of the puzzle, but nobody saw the
whole puzzle. So part of that is the hub.
And part of that is we've
initiated a hub within our national call center, which is
specifically dedicated to the young people
who have a parent that is
incarcerated, and making sure that we have sight of that
young person, making sure that they are safe.
Making sure that whoever is taking care of that young person as well, which is
a new approach,
that we're making sure that they have the supports they need to take care of that
young person until their parent comes out of the
corrections system. So rather than knocking on their door in a
real way of,
are these children safe? If they're not, here we come.
It's more of a knocking on the door and saying, "Have you got
everything that you need,
to take care of this young person? What can we do to help?"
And that hub helps to initiate that, because we have all the
agencies around the table to make those decisions and all the information,
every piece of the puzzle, is in one room.
So that's where some of the investment is going.
The other part is we know that our frontline social
workers have been under the pump for a very long time. It's not new.
It's probably been around since I was a young kid, where social workers
have talked about, now it's caseloads,
and the pressure of beating their statutory obligations.
So a lot of that funding will go into support for our
frontline social workers. And some of that will be in the role of the
allied support workers that we trialed, to work
alongside social workers to do the work that might not necessarily be
at a statutory level, but will take some of that pressure and that
load, so that social workers can concentrate on their statutory
work.
Minister, I might just-
Yeah
Thank you.
And just a couple, if we can just sort of short, quick questions last as I've got a

This was my primary at the beginning, and it's a very good start on this.
So, I am concerned that I hear about people

Yeah.
That's turned up three or four times to me.

Okay. So,
I too am concerned
about the situation that you're talking about, which is why we're putting such a
massive investment into making sure that
the area around reports of concern,
have more resourcing, have more training, and have more
capacity. And looking at ways where we can build in

talking about right now,
that doesn't happen. So yes, very concerned about it, but that's the
reason why all these things we've spoken about today around

is so important, and why we've put that major investment

Will we measure that matter of how many are being dropped out of the

That's a technical, so I'll just hand it over to Nicolette.
So I'll be very brief, but there are a range of things that we're doing both to
improve the way we make decisions at the front door and how we measure them.
So, we've introduced, for example, a digitized report of concern to make it
easier for people to report and to get really clear information and to be able to
use that information more clearly.
We've introduced a range of practice tools, which we've finalized at the end of
last year. We're seeing, because what we do is we look regularly at cases and do
case file review to make sure exactly that we understand
the trend that you're talking about.
So we've got one of those around our front door initial assessment, just about due
for completion at the moment. What it's showing us is that actually there's been a
significant improvement in the tools we used to make better decisions.
When they're used as intended, we see less of that.
But it's absolutely true that demand pressure
does have an impact, and so that's why the combination of things that the
minister's referred to, more allied support workers who can free up social work
time to do that initial assessment work, technology improvements in
our contact center, improvements in the way that we
redesign of a decision response tool, which is how we make those decisions in the
first place. All of that is gearing us up,
both to respond to the increase we have at the moment and looking ahead to what,
not just mandatory reporting might mean, but actually mandatory training.
So we want people to identify and report, and then we want to be able to respond.
Your question, Karanga.
Thank you, Mr. Chair.

Which, uh-

Well, when a young person gets removed and put into care,

A child in care can be in whānau care, so

And what are your ministers doing-
The majority of them are with whānau

Everything we do is trying to prevent a young

You cut prevention funding across the country.
No, we didn't.
You did.
So we are try— no, we didn't.
We are concentrating on making sure that the

And if that means intervention, and that means that we have to step in

And so the problem is,
